text: Increasing running step rate is associated with improvements in pain and function in recreational runners with patellofemoral pain at both 4 and 12 weeks.
specific_value: pain SMD 2.68 at 4 weeks, 3.62 at 12 weeks

text: Increasing running step rate reduces peak knee flexion angle with strong evidence.
Increasing running step rate reduces step length, peak hip adduction, peak knee extensor moment, and foot strike angle with moderate evidence.
specific_value: step length SMD 0.93, hip adduction SMD 0.40, knee extensor moment SMD 0.50, foot strike angle SMD 0.62

text: Increasing running step rate reduces braking impulse, peak hip flexion, and peak patellofemoral joint stress with limited evidence.
specific_value: braking impulse SMD 0.64, hip flexion SMD 0.42, patellofemoral stress SMD 0.56

text: Increasing step rate above preferred rate increases perceived exertion, awkwardness, effort, and metabolic energy consumption.
specific_value: exertion SMD -0.49, awkwardness SMD -0.72, effort SMD -0.69, metabolic cost SMD -0.84

text: Decreasing running step rate increases step length, contact time, braking impulse, and negative knee work.
specific_value: step length SMD -0.76, contact time SMD -0.95, braking impulse SMD -0.73, negative knee work SMD -0.88

text: Most evidence on step rate changes reflects only immediate effects; longer-term biomechanical and injury effects remain largely unknown.
